coredata.dc:title: PBC@cellulose-filter paper separator design with efficient ion transport properties toward stabilized zinc-ion battery
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Linbo
Peng, Jiaxin
Jia, Xuefeng
Zhu, Xiaojie
Meng, Bicheng
Yang, Kai
Chu, Dewei
Yang, Naixing
Yu, Juan
coredata.dc:description: The uneven pore structure and sluggish transport kinetics of the separator will accelerate the growth of dendrites and directly deteriorate the performance of zinc metal battery. Herein, we present a simple and unique interface engineering strategy to anchor persimmon branch carbon (PBC) material onto a cellulose-filter (CF) paper separator to address these issues. The novel PBC material demonstrates a hierarchical pore structure, uniform distribution of carbon dots, and rich N, and O functional groups, and an efficient ion transport modulator PBC@CF separator is designed to serve the dual function of promoting ions transfer and suppressing dendrite growth. Owing to the anchoring of PBC on CF and uniform filling of the surface interspace, the PBC modified separator not only greatly facilitates Zn2+ transport between the fiber-electrolyte interface, but also evenly redistributes the ion transport on the anode interface. As a result, highly reversible Zn plating/stripping is achieved in the symmetric Zn-Zn cell with the PBC@CF separator. Meanwhile, the modified Zn-LiFePO4 dual ion full battery can retain a specific capacity of 109.4 mAh g−1 after 200 cycles at 1 C with an excellent capacity retention of 86 %. The unique PBC material and facile separator engineering provide a new perspective to designing advanced separators for high-performance aqueous Zn dual ion full battery.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Separator engineering
Cellulose-filter paper separator
Efficient ion transport
Zinc-based dual ion battery

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (52034011, 51974222) and Natural Science Basic Research Plan in Shaanxi Province (2019JQ-764). The authors would like to thank Shiyanjia Lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for the SEM, XPS, Raman analysis and 3D Scientific Visualization (www.vismatrix.cn) for schematic model.

This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 52034011 , 51974222 ) and Natural Science Basic Research Plan in Shaanxi Province ( 2019JQ-764 ). The authors would like to thank Shiyanjia Lab ( www.shiyanjia.com ) for the SEM, XPS, Raman analysis and 3D Scientific Visualization ( www.vismatrix.cn ) for schematic model.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of the preparation of PBC@CF-separators and aqueous Zn-LiFePO4 dual ion full battery.
Morphology and structure of PBC samples. (a) (b) SEM images of PBC; (c) (d) TEM images of PBC; (e) (f) SEM image of vascular cambium in PBC samples;(g) (h) SEM image of cortex in PBC samples.

(a) SEM images of CF-separators; (b) SEM images of PBC@CF-separators; (c) Elemental mapping of C, N, and O elements of CF-separators; (d) Elemental mapping of C, N, and O elements of PBC@CF-separators.

(a) Raman spectroscopy of CF-separators and PBC@CF-separators; (b) Ionics conductivity of CF-separators and PBC@CF-separators; (c) XPS wide-spectra of CF-separators and PBC@CF-separators; The corresponding XPS spectra of (d) C 1s; (e) O 1s; (f) N 1s.

The Zn plating/stripping performance using CF separator and PBC@CF separator in the symmetric cell. Long-term cycling stability for the symmetrical cells with (a) 1 mA cm−2 and 0.5 mAh cm−2; (b) 2 mA cm−2 and 1 mAh cm−2; (c)Rate performance test for the symmetrical cells; (d) Linear polarization curves presenting the corrosion; (e) AC impedance analysis for the symmetrical cells; (f) Schematic diagram of the mechanism by the separator promotes ions transfer and suppresses dendrite growth.

SEM images at different magnifications of (aⅠ) (aⅡ)CF-separators after cycle in the Zn-Zn symmetric cell; (aⅢ) Elemental mapping of C, Zn, and O elements of CF-separators; (bⅠ) (bⅡ)PBC@CF-separators after cycle in the Zn-Zn symmetric cell; (bⅢ) Elemental mapping of C, Zn, and O elements of PBC@CF-separators; SEM images at different magnifications of (c)original Zn electrode; (d)Zn electrode after 20 cycles at 1mA cm−1 and 0.5mAh cm−1 in the Zn-Zn symmetric cell using CF-separators; (e)Zn electrode after 20 cycles at 1mA cm−1 and 0.5mAh cm−1in the Zn-Zn symmetric cell using PBC@CF-separators.

CV measurement of aqueous Zn-LiFePO4 dual ion full battery using (a) CF-separator and (b) PBC@CF-separator; (c) Li+ diffusion coefficient analyzed by the Randles-Sevcik Equation; (d) Nyquist plots of Zn-LiFePO4 dual ion full battery;(e) The linear relation of Impedance real-part and angular frequency; (f) Zn-LiFePO4 dual ion full battery of rate performance; (g) Cycling stability of Zn-LiFePO4 dual ion full battery at 1 C with 200 cycle number.

head.ce:title: PBC@cellulose-filter paper separator design with efficient ion transport properties toward stabilized zinc-ion battery

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These author contribute equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The uneven pore structure and sluggish transport kinetics of the separator will accelerate the growth of dendrites and directly deteriorate the performance of zinc metal battery. Herein, we present a simple and unique interface engineering strategy to anchor persimmon branch carbon (PBC) material onto a cellulose-filter (CF) paper separator to address these issues. The novel PBC material demonstrates a hierarchical pore structure, uniform distribution of carbon dots, and rich N, and O functional groups, and an efficient ion transport modulator PBC@CF separator is designed to serve the dual function of promoting ions transfer and suppressing dendrite growth. Owing to the anchoring of PBC on CF and uniform filling of the surface interspace, the PBC modified separator not only greatly facilitates Zn2+ transport between the fiber-electrolyte interface, but also evenly redistributes the ion transport on the anode interface. As a result, highly reversible Zn plating/stripping is achieved in the symmetric Zn-Zn cell with the PBC@CF separator. Meanwhile, the modified Zn-LiFePO4 dual ion full battery can retain a specific capacity of 109.4 mAh g−1 after 200 cycles at 1 C with an excellent capacity retention of 86 %. The unique PBC material and facile separator engineering provide a new perspective to designing advanced separators for high-performance aqueous Zn dual ion full battery.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon neutralization technology has become an important development goal in the world. Electrochemical storage technology is widely considered as an effective way to achieve this goal [1]. In recent years, the cost of lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) is expensive and the safety problem is gradually emerging [2]. Therefore, it is particularly important and urgent to develop a new energy storage system with low cost and high safety characteristics [3,4]. Zinc has high theoretical specific capacity (volume specific capacity of 5851 mAh cm−3 and mass specific capacity of 820 mAh g−1) and abundant resource reserves [5]. More importantly, aqueous zinc ion battery (AZIB) has the advantages of low cost and non-flammability compared with commercial organic LIB [6]. Therefore, AZIB shows great application prospect as a new generation of energy storage system [7,8]. However, the uneven deposition of Zn2+ on the electrode surface will lead to excessive dendrite growth, and the surface hydrogen evolution and undesirable side reactions will considerably reduce the battery life [9–11].

In general, separators play three important functions of isolating the contact of cathode and anode, blocking electrons, and transporting ions in the battery. The fast and uniform transport of zinc ions in the separator facilitates its further uniform deposition and reduces uncontrollable dendrite growth. Glass fiber (GF) filter paper [12] and cotton fiber filter paper are the most commonly used separators in AZIB [13], but they have serious problems such as low ionic conductivity, uneven pores, and easily to the growth of zinc dendrites [14]. Li et al. [15] propose a modification strategy for commercial GF separator that via directly growing vertical graphene carpet on one side to develop a Janus separator. Recently, a scalable Ti3C2Tx MXene-decorated Janus separator is designed by spray-printing MXene nanosheets over one side of commercial GF. Separator modification has recently blossomed as an effective strategy to enable dendrite-free Zn metal anodes [16]. Nafion-based membrane has been widely studied as the separator of AZIB owing to its high hydrophilicity, fast cation transfer characteristics, and high mechanical strength [17,18]. Ghosh et al. [19] found that the Nafion-based membrane has rich surface functional groups and good cation selectivity, which improves the cycle stability of AZIB. However, the practical application of Nafion membrane in batteries is limited by high price and complex pretreatment process [20]. Polyacrylonitrile membrane, polyolefin membrane, and other new separators show an excellent inhibition effect on dendrite growth. However, its application is impeded due to the cumbersome synthesis process and harsh synthesis environment [21]. In addition, Celgard 3501 membrane [22] can also be used in AZIB system, but there is a lack of detailed electrochemical reaction mechanism research. Zhang et al. achieved a high-performance dendrite-free aqueous zinc-ion battery by using a cost-effective weighing paper interlayer as an ion redistributor separator [23]. Therefore, it is very urgent to develop a low-cost, high-performance separator and reveal its working mechanism in AZIB. The cellulose-filter (CF) filter paper is usually used for the filtration of water-based solutions, and has wide applications in medicine, chemistry, food and other fields. Therefore, using CF filter paper as separator of AZIB battery is a good choice from the perspective of cost. However, CF filter paper has the characteristics of large and uneven pore structure, which will lead to slow ion transport and uncontrolled dendrite growth. The slow ion transport process severely restricts the development of AZIB. Consequently, we proposed an interface engineering design strategy for the separator to modify CF filter paper to enhance its ionic conductivity and homogenize the zinc deposition process.

In this work, firstly, hierarchically porous persimmon branch carbon (PBC) with uniformly distributed carbon dots was prepared by a one-step carbonization method. Secondly, a design strategy of separator interface engineering is proposed to modify CF filter paper using a simple filtration method. The PBC@CF filter paper separator was prepared by anchoring PBC carbon particles on CF filter paper. The PBC porous materials have hierarchical pore structure, surface distribution of carbon dots and rich N, O functional groups, which makes the separator has high wettability and zincophilicity [24,25]. The PBC modified separator acts as an ion accelerator to promote ion transport at the fiber-electrolyte interface. In addition, the uneven macropores in the CF separator are filled with PBC particles, which makes the interface layer more uniform as an ion redistribution device, and ensures the uniformity of the electric field distribution at the separator-anode interface [15]. Compared with the CF separator, the PBC@CF separator designed by interface engineering exhibits longer electrochemical durability, more uniform and dense network structure, and better ion transport efficiency. Therefore, the PBC@CF separator uniformizes the Zn2+ ion flux, promotes the ion transfer, and reduces the ions de-solvation energy, eventually lowering the nucleation overpotential and accelerating the deposition kinetics. In practical application, the Zn-Zn symmetric battery assembled with PBC@CF separator shows better cycle stability than CF separator. In addition, high-performance LiFePO4-Zn dual-ion battery was also realized by using PBC@CF separator. The whole preparation process of modified separator and high-performance dual-ion battery is shown in Fig. 1 .

Zinc sulfate (ZnSO4.7H2O, AR), Lithium sulfate (Li2SO4.H2O, AR), zinc foil (Qirui Metal Material Co., Ltd., 30 µm), stainless steel foil (20 µm), polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF, AR), methylmercuric iodide pyrrolidone (NMP, GC), and conductive carbon black (Shenzhen Kejingstar Technology, Ltd.) were purchased from the Macklin Chemical Company. The cellulose filter paper separators were used (JIAO JIE, Fushun Civil Affairs Filter Paper Factory, NO.GB/T1914-2017). Deionized water was produced using the laboratory water purification system of Hltech Instruments Co., Ltd. Each material was used directly, without further purification. Shattered dried persimmon branches were placed in a tube furnace after drying at 120 °C for 12 h and carbonized under a nitrogen atmosphere at 600 °C for 2 h. Persimmon branches carbonized at 600 °C were denoted as persimmon branch carbon (PBC).

Firstly, 100 mg PBC and 10 mg sodium carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC, CP, 300∼800 mPa s) were respectively added into 100 ml H2O, then ultrasonic for 2 h to obtain the uniform dispersed solution. Secondly, PBC can be decorated on cellulose filter paper separators through vacuum extraction and filtration. Finally, PBC modified cellulose filter paper separator can be obtained after vacuum drying at 100 °C for 24 h. In detail, the load of 1.76–2.11 mg cm−2 could be obtained by adding 100 mg PBC and 10 mg CMC into the H2O solution.

The Raman spectroscopy (InVia RM200 Renishaw) with a 532 nm diode laser between, focusing between 250 and 3750 cm−1. The morphologies and microstructures of the samples were observed using scanning electron microscopy (SEM, TESCAN MIRA LMS) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEOL JEM-2100). The superficial chemical compositions of the samples were studied using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Scientific K-Alpha). The ionic conductivity was tested before and after separator modification by the Four-probe tester (FT-8100).

To prepare the cathode, LiFePO4, Super-P and polyvinylidene fluoride(PVDF) were mixed at a mass ratio of 8:1:1. N-Methyl pyrrolidinone(NMP) was added into the mixture and the slurry was coated onto stainless steel foil. The average active material loading of LiFePO4 was approximately 1.2–1.8 mg cm−2. Before assembling the battery, all the electrodes were dried in a vacuum oven at 120 °C for 12 h. The 1 M ZnSO4 aqueous electrolyte was used in the symmetrical cell test. The 2 M ZnSO4 and 7 M Li2SO4 mixed aqueous electrolyte was used in the Zn-LiFePO4 dual ion full battery test. CR2025 cell battery were assembled in air with an ambient humidity of 30–50 %. Galvanostatic charge-discharge (GCD) tests were carried out using a Neware battery test system (CT-4008T-5V20mA-164) at 25 °C. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements were carried out on a PARSTAT 4000A (Ametek US) and CHI660E electrochemical station.

The PBC biomass carbon material prepared by one-step carbonization was characterized by SEM and TEM. Fig. 2 a and b correspond to the SEM images of the PBC at different magnifications, and Fig. 2c and d correspond to the TEM images of the PBC samples at different magnifications. The PBC samples showed a distinct natural skeletal structure characteristic of biomass. Under the high magnification, large number of microsphere particles can be observed uniformly attached to the surface of the carbon skeleton. In the TEM test, it can be observed that PBC has a porous structure, and there are uniform carbon dots with a size of 3–5 nm [26,27]. The cortex and vascular cambium domains of persimmon branches were mechanically separated, and then carbonization treatment showed that the two morphologies were completely different. As shown in Fig. 2e and f, the carbon material displayed a hierarchical tube wall structure characteristic of the vascular cambium. As shown in Fig. 2g and h, the cortex of persimmon branches showed large number of microsphere structures after carbonization, which may be the source of carbon dots in the PBC samples.

As illustrated in Fig. 3 , the morphology of CF-separators and PBC@CF-separators were characterized. As seen in Fig. 3a, the cellulose filter is made up of numerous messy winding fibers with different sizes and lengths, resulting in an irregular pore size distribution. In contrast to the pure CF-separator, the surface morphology of modified PBC@CF-separators was found to an evolution. In detail, PBC microparticles would initially anchor on the cellulose fiber as shown in Fig. 3b. SEM-energy dispersive spectrometer (EDS) mapping of the CF-separators over the highlighted area displays the presence of C and O elements in Fig. 3c. However, the EDS mapping of the PBC@CF-separators area displays the obviously C, O, and N elements as shown in Fig. 3d. The elemental mapping further confirms the uniform distribution of PBC microparticles within the CF-separators. In addition, the excess PBC microparticles would tend to gather and fill the interspace between the CF-separators surface. As a result, the well decoration of PBC on cellulose fiber is observed as well as the uniform filling of surface interspace, as shown in Fig. 3b. Generally, a separator with a well-distributed pore is beneficial to ion transportation, inducing a uniform metal stripping/plating process [28]. Mercury intrusion analysis tests were performed to further clarify the pore size distribution of the separators. Fig. S1 shows the Cumulative Intrusion vs Pressure curve and Pore distribution map of the two samples. The pore size distribution of the separator was reduced from 9000 nm to 1000 nm by the addition of PBC, and pores smaller than 100 nm appeared obviously. PBC plays the role of filling the macropores and reducing the tortuosity of the separator from 48.196 to 5.498 without affecting the porosity. The curved channels that are not conducive to transport are filled with PBC, and its porous structure is more conducive to ion transport. PBC@CF can effectively enhance the electrolyte penetration and improve the transport efficiency during the electrochemical reaction [29,30]. The detailed specific surface areas and pore channel information for two separators sample are listed in Table S1.

Fig. 4 a illustrates a comparison of the Raman spectra of PBC@CF-separators and CF-separators. The CF-separators have positive peaks at locations that could be attributed to crystalline cellulose: 380 cm−1, 1,000–1150 cm−1, 1300–1500 cm−1, 2894 cm−1 and 3357 cm−1. This is consistent with studies on celluloses reported in the literature [31,32]. The Raman peaks located at 2600–3500 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibration mode of CH and OH. The Raman peaks located at 1000–1300 cm−1 mainly correspond to the CC stretching and COH and CCH deformation vibrations [33]. The characteristic peaks of 1350 cm−1, 1590 cm−1, and 2660 cm−1 can be obviously observed on the Raman spectra of PBC@CF-separators, representing the surface disorder, graphitized structures, and 2D band of the carbon, respectively [34]. This is considered as a typical CC vibration peak, confirming that PBC microparticles would successfully anchor on the CF-separators and improve the surface composition [35]. Fig. S2 shows the good wettability of the separator to the electrolyte, and the results show that PBC does not affect the wettability of the separator. In addition, the conductivity of PBC@CF-separators and CF-separators were 17.63 mS cm−1 and 8.85 mS cm−1in Fig. 4b, respectively. The much higher conductivity of the modified separator was attributed to the PBC material.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were conducted to further compare the composition and the chemical states of PBC@CF-separators and CF-separators. As shown in Fig. 4c, the signal of elements C, N, O, and S can be observed in both XPS survey spectrum spectra. The modification of the separator with PBC significantly increased the content of C and N on the surface of the separator, which also proved the improvement of the conductivity of the PBC@CF-separators [36,37]. Moreover, the changes in the surface chemical state of the element are analyzed by comparing the high-resolution spectra of C1s, O1s and N1s before and after surface modification. In the C 1s spectrum shown in Fig. 4d, the peak at 284.8 eV, 285.7 eV, 287.1 eV, and 288.2 eV could be assigned to CC/CH [38], CO/CN [39,40], CO [41], and OCO [42] bonding, respectively. Furthermore, the presence of CO, CO [43], OCO, and HOH [44] bonds are also evidenced in the O 1s spectrum in Fig. 4e. The high-resolution N 1s profile can be deconvoluted into three contributive signals in Fig. 4f, corresponding to CN (398.1 eV), NH2 (400.5 eV) [42], pyrrolic-N (401.6 eV), and oxidized-N (403.7 eV) [45]. This is ultimately beneficial to the electrolyte wetting and Zn-ion binding, which results in high Zn-ion transport rate. Note that the engineering of fiber-electrolyte interface could be expected to act as ions accelerator to efficiently redistribute the ion transport into homogenization and boost the ion transport. Through such separator design, efficient modulation of ions transport can be achieved for stable Zn metal anodes.

Subsequently, the electrochemical performance and chemical stability of the two separators were characterized in Fig. 5 . In the long-term cycling test with a current density of 1 mA cm−1, the cycle life of the Zn-PBC@CF separator in the symmetric cell in Fig. 5a is more than 322 h, while the CF separator symmetric cell can only work for 110 h. Identically, the PBC@CF separator has longer cycle stability than the CF separator in the cycling test with a high current density of 2 mA cm−1 in Fig. 5b. During the long-term cycling test, the cell with the PBC@CF separator holds a lower voltage hysteresis with respect to that with the CF separator. The Zn symmetric cells with different separators were also galvanostatic cycled at current densities from 0.5 to 4 mA cm−2 in Fig. 5c. Obviously, the voltage hysteresis values with the PBC@CF separator are below those with the CF separator throughout all the current densities. Moreover, Fig. 5d shows that the exchange current density of cell based on PBC@CF-separator obtained from the corrosion plots is also much larger than that of the CF-separator-based cell, further demonstrating the accelerated Zn2+ transport kinetics on the electrode surface of the modified cell [46]. Fig. 5e shows that the radius of curvature of the PBC@CF-separators is much smaller than that of the CF-separators, indicating a lower ions transference impedance in the zinc symmetric cell using PBC@CF-separators. This is due to that the electronegativity of N and O atoms in PBC repels the SO4 2−, which limits the migration of anion and promotes the cation transference [47]. Fig. 5f illustrates the accelerated ion transport and Inhibited zinc dendrite growth by the PBC@CF separator in Zn-Zn symmetric cells. The PBC modification reduces the tortuosity and improves the ionic conductivity of the CF separator, which makes the ion transport process faster and more uniform. The fast and uniform ion transport process will benefit the uniform nucleation of Zn ions, thereby reducing growth and fall off the unstable dendrite.

The improvement of the undesirable phenomenon of dendrite growth on the zinc electrode surface by separator modification was further evaluated, and the reasons for the improved cycling performance were also elucidated. Fig. S3 shows the optical images of the separator and zinc metal electrode. Some black spots appeared in the cycled CF separator, which was caused by the shedding of zinc dendrites as shown in Fig. S3b. The corrosion of the Zn metal electrode with CF separator is more severe and non-uniform compared with that of the Zn metal electrode with PBC@CF separator. Fig. 6 a and b correspond to the CF-separator and PBC@CF-separator after 20 cycles at 1 mA cm−1 and 0.5 mAh cm−1 in the Zn-Zn symmetric cell, respectively. After 20 cycles in the Zn-Zn symmetric cell, some flakes of hydrated hexagonal zinc dendrites already emerge on the surface of the CF-separator as shown in Fig. 6aⅡ [48–50]. No obvious hexagonal crystal Zn protrusions are observed on the PBC@CF-separators by separator modification as shown in Fig. 6bⅡ. It can also be analyzed from the elemental mapping tests in Fig. 6aⅢ and bⅢ that the surface protruding particles correspond to zinc dendrites and PBC, respectively. Morphology evolution of Zinc deposition on bare was further investigated to reveal the effect of separator modification on Zinc metal anode as shown in Fig. 6c–e. The original Zn electrode has a flat surface structure and the inherent cracks and scratches generated by the production process as shown in Fig. 6c. The CF-separator has the characteristics of a large and uneven pore structure, which results in the irregular distribution of current density and electrolyte concentration on the electrode surface during the charging and discharging and hence leads to serious dendrite growth on the electrode surface as shown in Fig. 6d. The PBC modified separator significantly improves the deposition morphology of zinc ions, and no hexagonal crystal Zn dendrite protrusions are formed on Zn electrode as shown in Fig. 6e.

In order to further evaluate the influence of PBC interlayers on the electrochemical performance and the feasibility in practical application, aqueous Zn-LiFePO4 dual ion full battery was assembled and tested for comparison. The CV curve of different rates in Fig. 7 a and b were carried out to explore the change of reaction kinetics before and after PBC separator modification. For LiFePO4 electrodes, the CV curves obtained before and after PBC separator modification at different scan rates were similar in shape. The response was determined by Randles-Sevcik equation [51–53], as follows:
(1)
Ip
=
0
. 4463 nFAC
(
nFvD / RT
)
1 / 2
=
(
(
269000
)
n
3 / 2
A
D
1 / 2
C
)
v
1 / 2
where Ip is the peak current of the cyclic voltammogram, n is the number of electrons transferred during the redox process (n=2), F is the Faraday constant (C mol−1), A is the area of the electrode (A=1.54 cm2), C is the concentration of Zn (C=0.001 mol cm−3), D is the diffusion coefficient (cm2 s−1), R is the gas constant, and T is the absolute temperature. As shown in Fig. 6c, the slope is equivalent to the anodic peak current Ip versus the square root of scan rate (v1/2), which the diffusion coefficient determines electrochemical reaction rate. Based on the fit line, the slope of aqueous Zn-LiFePO4 dual ion full battery using CF-separator and PBC@CF-separator were 1.61 and 1.70, respectively. The averages of the diffusion coefficients in the aqueous Zn-LiFePO4 dual ion full battery using CF-separator and PBC@CF-separator were ultimately determined as D CF-separator = 1.88 × 10−6, and D PBC@CF-separator = 2.11 × 10−6, respectively.

Furthermore, the Electrochemical impedance spectrum (EIS) was employed to reveal the influence of PBC separator modification on the transport kinetics during cycling. The impedance changing trends for aqueous Zn-LiFePO4 dual ion full battery using CF-separator and PBC@CF-separator are similar, while the overall impedance of PBC@CF-separator is smaller than that of CF-separator in Fig. 7d. It illustrates that the modification of PBC to the separator accelerates and uniformizes the ion transport process, thereby reducing the ion transport resistance at the electrode interface. The modification of the separator by PBC not only reduces the transport resistance of the interface but also accelerates the diffusion of ions in the electrode body. The low-frequency Warburg impedance corresponding mainly to the diffusion of ions in the bulk of the electrode has been used to determine the ion diffusion coefficient. The lithium-ion diffusion coefficients can be calculated from the formula as follows [54]:
(2)
D
=
R
2
T
2
2
A
2
n
4
F
4
C
2
σ
2

Where R=8.314 J K−1 mol−1 is the gas constant, T=298 K is the room temperature in our experiment, A=1.54 × 10−4 m2 is the surface area of the electrode, n=1 is the number of the electrons per molecule attending the electronic transfer reaction, F=96,500 C mol−1 is the Faraday constant, C=0.42 × 103 mol m−3 is the concentration of lithium ion, σ is the slope in Fig. 7e, respectively. The calculated lithium diffusion coefficient of the aqueous Zn-LiFePO4 dual ion full battery using CF-separator and PBC@CF-separator were D
CF-separator
=6.12 × 10−15 cm2 s−1, and D
PBC@CF-separator
=1.79 × 10−14 cm2 s−1, respectively. In order to ensure the electrical neutrality of the system, the cathode intercalates two lithium ions, and the anode dissolves one zinc ion. Therefore, the ion transference number of Zn2+ should be half that of Li+, and the transference rate should be twice that of Li+. Fig. 7f is the rate performance test of the aqueous Zn-LiFePO4 dual-ion full battery using CF-separator and PBC@CF-separator. The aqueous Zn-LiFePO4 dual ion full battery using PBC@CF-separator sample showed the specific capacities of 173.1, 156.1, 137.1, 104, 73.4, 52.2, and 41 mAh g−1 at the current densities of 0.5 C, 1 C, 2 C, 5 C, 10 C, 20 C, and 30 C, respectively (1 C=170 mA g−1). It was illustrated that the modification of the separator by PBC can help the electrode material to perform more fully in the ability of intercalation and deionization under the condition of low current (0.5 C), so the capacity is more excellent. PBC improves the electrical conductivity and accelerates the ion diffusion under high current conditions (5 C). Under ultra-high current conditions (30 C), the limiting bottleneck of capacity lies in the interfacial diffusion and internal transport rate of the electrode material itself, so the separator modification cannot improve battery performance at this magnitude. Therefore, the Zn-LiFePO4 batteries using CF-separator and PBC@CF-separator have basically the same capacity of about 41 mAh g−1 at 30 C current density. Finally, the cyclic stability of the battery after the rate test was evaluated at the current density of 1 C, as shown in Fig. 7g. Compared with the capacity retention of 67 % after 200 cycles for normal battery, this value can be significantly improved up to 86 % after PBC separator modification. Table S2 provides a detailed comparison of the electrochemical performance of the PBC@CF-separator with other separators in full cells. Preliminary experiments in this study confirm that the interfacial engineering method of loading porous carbon into the separator can effectively improve the transport efficiency of the separator and limit dendrite growth. Based on this manuscript, extensive research can be carried on the pore structure, specific surface area, functional group characteristics, loading, and electrolyte synergistic for porous carbon in the future. Porous carbon has broad research prospects as a separator modification material.

In summary, we have fabricated a kind of novel PBC carbon-based material with a hierarchical pore structure and proposed a simple and unique interface engineering strategy, which anchors PBC on a CF paper separator to enhance the ion transfer. It was investigated that PBC@CF separator could promote the uniform zinc deposition and retardate the growth of zinc dendrites from the perspective of the separator in details. The modified PBC@CF separator increases the ionic conductivity from 8.85 to 17.63 mS cm−1, reduces the zinc nucleation overpotential, raises the exchange current density from 4.78 × 10−2 to 7.32 × 10−2 mA cm−2, and improves the zinc stripping/plating reversibility compared to CF separator. The zinc symmetrical cell with PBC@CF separator can cycle stably over 300 h at a current density of 1 mA cm−2, which is about three times of the cell with CF separator. With the PBC@CF separator, the Zn-LiFePO4 dual ion full battery exhibits great rate capability and more importantly, significantly improved cycling stability over 200 cycles with 86 % of initial capacity maintained at 1 C in comparison with the batteries with CF separator. Based on a kind of hierarchical pore structure PBC carbon-based material, the AZIB system was optimized through a simple and unique interface engineering strategy to achieve the advantages of non-toxicity, high power, and long-cycle stability, thereby promoting the application of high-performance AZIB.

Linbo Li: Supervision, Methodology, Resources. Jiaxin Peng: Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing, Data curation. Xuefeng Jia: Visualization, Data curation. Xiaojie Zhu: Visualization. Bicheng Meng: Validation, Software. Kai Yang: Investigation, Validation. Dewei Chu: Conceptualization, Resources. Naixing Yang: Methodology, Resources. Juan Yu: Methodology, Formal analysis, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (52034011, 51974222) and Natural Science Basic Research Plan in Shaanxi Province (2019JQ-764). The authors would like to thank Shiyanjia Lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for the SEM, XPS, Raman analysis and 3D Scientific Visualization (www.vismatrix.cn) for schematic model.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.electacta.2022.141129.
